planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in spring; showy goldenrod is one of the most garden-worthy of all native goldenrods — less aggressively spreading than common goldenrod and producing more upright, elegant flower plumes.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; showy goldenrod is one of the most ornamentally refined of all native goldenrods — producing stiffly erect, clump-forming stems (rather than the invasive rhizomatous spread of S. canadensis) topped in late summer and fall by large, dense, pyramidal plumes of vivid golden-yellow flowers that provide the most intense and lasting goldenrod bloom display available and are exceptionally attractive to monarch butterflies, native bees, and late-migrating birds seeking seed. Its more controlled growth habit and superior flower presentation make it a far more garden-worthy selection than common goldenrod for the formal border and prairie garden. It does not cause hay fever — the pollen is too heavy to be wind-dispersed and is spread only by insects. Thrives in average to lean soil and tolerates drought well once established; rich, fertile, moist soil encourages excessive vegetative growth at the expense of flowering.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds and soak in warm water 12 hours before direct sowing in average to poor, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; showy tick trefoil fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and improves poor, degraded soils.

directions: Scarify seeds and soak in warm water 12 hours before direct sowing in average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; showy tick trefoil is a tall, robust native prairie legume that produces large, branching panicles of small, rosy-pink, pea-like flowers from July through August — one of the most generously blooming of all native prairie wildflowers in its season and exceptional for attracting a wide diversity of native bees, bumble bees, and butterflies. As a legume, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and actively improves impoverished prairie and meadow soils. Seed capsules break into individual hooked segments that cling to fabric and animal fur for dispersal — the classic 'tick trefoil' seed. The large, compound, three-part leaves are attractive through the growing season. Excellent for native plant meadow restorations, prairie gardens, and wildlife habitat plantings where its late summer bloom fills a critical pollinator-support gap.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in average to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately well-drained soil in full sun in early spring; stratify seeds cold for 30 days in the refrigerator before sowing to improve germination rates. Sullivan's milkweed does not transplant well once established due to a deep taproot — plant it where it can remain permanently.

directions: Direct sow stratified seeds in average to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sullivan's milkweed (smooth milkweed) is a superior native milkweed alternative to common milkweed (A. syriaca) for garden settings — it produces the same essential monarch butterfly breeding habitat but in a more refined, clump-forming growth habit without the aggressive rhizomatous spread that makes common milkweed difficult to manage in ornamental borders. The smooth, hairless, blue-green leaves and stems are handsomely architectural, and large, fragrant, rosy-pink flower clusters appear in June and July — slightly more ornamental than the common milkweed. The inflated, warted seed pods split in fall to release silky-plumed seeds. Considered an excellent monarch host plant for formal and semiformal garden settings where common milkweed's spread would be problematic. Less common in the nursery trade than some milkweeds but increasingly available from native plant specialists.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; royal catchfly resents clay and wet soils — excellent drainage is critical. The plant blooms the second year from seed and is very long-lived once established.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; royal catchfly is a spectacular native Midwestern prairie catchfly with intensely vivid, cardinal-red, five-petaled flowers with deeply notched petals that appear on branching stems from June through August — the color is among the most brilliant scarlet-red available in a hardy native perennial. Ruby-throated hummingbirds are powerfully attracted to the flowers and are the primary pollinators. The plant requires excellent drainage and will not tolerate wet or heavy clay soil; it thrives in typical rocky Midwestern prairie conditions. It is considered a rare plant in the wild and is declining across its native range — garden cultivation helps preserve the species. An outstanding plant for hot, dry, rocky prairie gardens and hummingbird borders. Combines beautifully with native prairie dropseed, little bluestem, and wild bergamot in dry, naturalistic plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained to consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade in spring; seeds require cold stratification (60-90 days at 35°F) to germinate — sow outdoors in fall for natural stratification or refrigerate moist seeds before indoor sowing.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; bottle gentian (closed gentian) is one of the most unusual and intriguing of all native North American wildflowers — the flowers never open. Deep navy-blue to violet, bottle-shaped flowers are clustered at the tops of leafy stems in late August through October, and they remain permanently closed — only large bumblebees are strong enough to force the petals open to access the nectar and pollen within, making them one of the few plants that have evolved an exclusively bumblebee-pollinated flower mechanism. The deep-blue color and late-season bloom make it one of the last truly vivid wildflowers of autumn, blooming alongside native asters and goldenrods. Requires consistently moist, slightly acidic soil and does not tolerate drought. An excellent plant for moist shade gardens, rain gardens, and stream-side plantings where its unique late-season flower adds genuine surprise and interest.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
